critical_findings.title: Vendor reseller-permission AND real per-tenant isolation are both load-bearing, both unverified, and gated too late — the whole Phase-B build could be wrong
critical_findings.argument: The plan's entire L1 architecture and its named residual existential risk (vendor/Google/MS killing the master account over aggregate abuse) rest on two unconfirmed counterparty assumptions. (1) Inboxkit's public ToS positions it as a reseller that is 'not liable for outcomes' with the account holder 'responsible for all activities that occur under their account.' A single master account fronting many customers means EpiphanyMade absorbs 100% of downstream abuse liability to Inboxkit, and the ToS is not confirmed to even PERMIT multi-tenant sub-provisioning/reselling. (2) A competitor review asserts Inboxkit's 'isolation' is a UI domain-panel, not separate Google/M365 orgs per tenant. If true, SPEC §7's central claim ('there is no shared reputation pool to protect') is FALSE — one spammer tenant can bleed into co-tenants and flag the master account, resurrecting the exact shared-reputation-death risk the SPEC dismisses. The ROADMAP puts these checks in Phase 0/§16 (economics-framed) rather than as a Phase-A gate, so B1–B7 get built against a vendor model that may be contractually forbidden or that lacks the isolation the design depends on. Failure scenario: weeks of Phase-B build completed, then at activation Inboxkit refuses reseller use or reveals shared-org sending, invalidating the isolation model and the abuse-containment story simultaneously.
critical_findings.fix: Make written vendor confirmation a Phase-A gate that BLOCKS Phase B: obtain in writing from Inboxkit (a) that master/reseller multi-tenant sub-provisioning is permitted under an explicit reseller/agency agreement, (b) the exact isolation boundary — separate Google/M365 organizational tenants per customer vs shared org, and (c) sanctions/AUP pass-through and liability terms. Qualify SPEC §7's 'no shared reputation pool' as conditional on verified per-tenant org isolation and add a documented fallback (Zapmail/Mailreef dedicated-IP) if isolation proves cosmetic. Confirm a second vendor to the same gate so the facade's vendor-swap story is real, not theoretical.

critical_findings.title: Per-tenant advertiser physical address + verified sender identity are missing from the data model and tool surface — every real cold email will be CAN-SPAM non-compliant, and sandbox-first guarantees it's never caught until activation
critical_findings.argument: CAN-SPAM requires each commercial email carry the physical postal address of the SENDER/advertiser and accurately identify that sender. In this multi-tenant model the advertiser is the CUSTOMER, so the required address is the customer's, injected into every footer — EpiphanyMade's single address (SPEC §0.2) covers only EpiphanyMade's OWN marketing, not the millions of cold emails sent on customers' behalf. §0.8 lists 'physical address' as a guardrail without specifying whose; the natural reading (EpiphanyMade's one address) is non-compliant for tenant sends. The §6 tool surface captures brand/domains/persona but NO physical address, legal entity name, or verified sender identity. Compounding this: the 'customer is the sender' liability posture requires WHOIS/registrant/mailbox identity to actually identify the customer; if lookalike domains are bought under EpiphanyMade's registrar account with EpiphanyMade WHOIS, EpiphanyMade becomes a co-'initiator' and shares CAN-SPAM liability. Failure scenario (silent): sandbox-first never sends real mail, so the missing address/identity fields surface in zero tests; the product ships 'compliant' and the first live batch at activation is illegal on the address + sender-ID requirements across every send.
critical_findings.fix: Add mandatory per-tenant onboarding fields — legal entity/sender name, valid physical postal address, verified sending identity — validated before any activation; the sequencing engine injects the tenant's address into every footer. Make the sandbox exercise the REAL footer-rendering path and assert the tenant address is present, so the gap fails a test in test mode. Register lookalike domains to the customer (or WHOIS clearly identifying the customer) and operationally document the customer-as-sender designation, not just assert it in ToS. Add physical-address + sender-identity params to setup_infrastructure and a compliance-status field to infrastructure_status.

critical_findings.title: 'US-first CAN-SPAM' omits the state deceptive-email statutes that actually carry a private right of action and per-email statutory damages — and the auto-lookalike-domain + AI subject lines are the exact triggers
critical_findings.argument: CAN-SPAM has NO private right of action (only FTC/AG/ISP enforce), so 'US-first CAN-SPAM' understates the real litigation exposure. California B&P 17529.5 gives ANY recipient a private right of action at $1,000 per email (up to $1M per incident) for falsified/misrepresented/forged header information or a subject line likely to mislead about a material fact, plus attorney's fees — with a safe harbor reducing damages to $100/email only if the sender 'established and implemented practices reasonably designed to effectively prevent' violations. Plaintiff firms (e.g., Pacific Trial Attorneys) run 17529.5 demand-letter/class-action campaigns at scale. This platform auto-generates lookalike sending domains and lets the customer's agent write subject lines the platform does not constrain — precisely the header/subject-deception vectors 17529.5 targets — across thousands of sends, making both the customer AND the platform (as facilitator) exposed. Failure scenario: a customer's agent picks a lookalike that doesn't clearly identify the sender or writes a misleading subject; a CA recipient sues at $1,000/email; because the platform built no prevention practices, the $100 safe harbor doesn't apply.
critical_findings.fix: Extend the compliance layer beyond CAN-SPAM to state deceptive-email statutes (CA 17529.5 at minimum). Enforce in the engine, as hard validators: a sender-identifying From/domain that clearly maps to the tenant's real identity; a subject-line honesty guardrail on agent-supplied subjects (reject material misrepresentation); and a lookalike-generator validator that only produces domains reading as the TENANT's own brand and rejects any third-party brand (tighten SPEC §8's soft rule into a hard reject). Document these as the 'reasonable practices' that trigger the 17529.5 safe harbor. Add optional geo-suppression and a ToS rep that the customer owns the brand it is a lookalike of.

critical_findings.title: 'Light KYC + card-on-file' cannot protect the master account, and the complaint-monitoring backstop is structurally blind at per-tenant volume
critical_findings.argument: The one residual existential risk the SPEC names (vendor/Google/MS terminating the master account over aggregate abuse) is defended by 'per-tenant complaint monitoring + auto-pause + light KYC + ToS.' Each leg is weak: (1) card-on-file + light KYC does not stop a stolen-card/throwaway-identity spammer from onboarding and pointing a spam cannon at scraped lists; (2) Gmail/Google Postmaster and complaint-rate signals require minimum sending volume to report — at a few isolated mailboxes per tenant the 0.30% complaint signal is noisy, delayed, or absent, so 'auto-pause on complaint threshold' fires only AFTER the damage that flags the master account; (3) there is no OFAC/denied-party screening, so providing infrastructure to a sanctioned party is itself illegal. Detection is weakest exactly where prevention is needed. Failure scenario: a motivated spammer signs up with a burner card, blasts a purchased list from isolated domains; per-tenant complaint data never reaches statistical threshold in time; Inboxkit/Google flag the aggregate pattern on the master account before auto-pause triggers.
critical_findings.fix: Define an onboarding friction ladder gating real sends: business-email domain verification, Stripe Radar + AVS/3DS card checks, OFAC/denied-party screen, prohibited-use click-through, and list-provenance attestation (no scraped/purchased lists). Add a new-tenant volume ramp with a first-batch review before uncapping. Use seed-list/spam-trap hits and reply-rate/bounce heuristics as LEADING indicators since per-tenant complaint data is thin. Prevention-first, because detection is structurally weak at per-tenant scale.

critical_findings.title: ToS/AUP/Privacy is a checkbox deliverable with the load-bearing clauses unspecified — and an unenforced 'abuse-drop ToS' is FTC facilitator exposure, not protection
critical_findings.argument: ROADMAP D3 lists 'ToS, Privacy, AUP (CAN-SPAM-forward, DRAFT for attorney review)' but specifies none of the clauses that determine whether a cold-email platform survives, so the draft can silently omit them. Missing-clause failure scenarios: without a customer-is-the-CAN-SPAM-sender designation + reps, EpiphanyMade is a co-sender on every violation; without indemnification, EpiphanyMade eats recipient claims and regulatory actions from customer misuse; without explicit consent to content/metadata monitoring, the complaint-scanning/auto-pause loop itself may be unlawful interception; without a no-deliverability-warranty + limitation of liability, every under-delivering campaign is a breach claim; without Inboxkit + Google/MS AUP pass-through, the vendor terms don't bind the customer. Separately, the FTC can hold a service provider liable for knowingly facilitating others' CAN-SPAM/deceptive violations (means-and-instrumentalities), so an AUP that exists only on paper — with no built, tested terminate path — is not a defense. Auto-renewal on card-on-file also triggers state ARL (e.g., California) + FTC ROSCA disclosure/easy-cancel duties (note: the federal 'Click-to-Cancel' rule was vacated July 2025, but state ARLs and ROSCA still apply).
critical_findings.fix: Specify the mandatory clause inventory BEFORE drafting (see missing_items) and require the enforcement path (auto-pause/terminate) be built and tested, not just documented. Must-haves: customer-is-sender designation + reps (owns list, valid physical address, accurate identity, brand rights for lookalikes); indemnification of EpiphanyMade; Inboxkit + Google/MS AUP pass-through binding the customer as if direct; explicit consent to content/metadata monitoring; no-deliverability-warranty + limitation of liability; prohibited-use list (scraped/purchased lists, third-party impersonation, phishing, filter-evasion, illegal content, sanctioned parties) with immediate suspension; auto-renewal/negative-option disclosure compliant with state ARLs + ROSCA; and a privacy policy + processor/DPA addendum (controller vs processor for uploaded lead data — CCPA/CPRA reach CA residents even in a US-first plan).

critical_findings.title: Publishing marketing NOW carries deceptive-availability, unsubstantiated-deliverability, and AI-washing FTC traps, and triggers EpiphanyMade's own CAN-SPAM/privacy duties on waitlist email
critical_findings.argument: Publishing public surfaces early is a locked decision and fine; the risk is the COPY. Present-tense capability framing ('your agent runs cold email end-to-end') on a site with zero live backend, while collecting waitlist signups, risks a deceptive-availability claim. Any inbox-placement/deliverability promise is an unsubstantiated-efficacy claim — the SPEC is admirably honest internally (§9/§10: warmup depreciating, no magic) but that honesty must propagate to public copy or it's an FTC substantiation problem, plus AI-washing risk from overstating what 'AI runs it' means. Worst: framing the 'AI human-mimicry warmup' as a way to 'get past spam filters' reads as filter-evasion — both an FTC facilitation flag and a reputational/vendor-optics problem. And collecting waitlist emails makes EpiphanyMade itself a sender subject to CAN-SPAM + privacy law. Failure scenario: early-access site promises capability/deliverability, collects emails with no privacy policy, and an early-access blast goes out without an opt-out or address — EpiphanyMade violates the very law its product is supposed to help customers follow.
critical_findings.fix: Gate all present-tense capability claims behind an unambiguous 'in development / not yet available / no live sending' disclosure; no deliverability or inbox guarantees (propagate the SPEC's internal honesty verbatim); frame warmup as legitimate reputation-building, never filter-evasion; publish a privacy policy before collecting a single email and make EpiphanyMade's own early-access emails CAN-SPAM-compliant (address + opt-out); collect no payment pre-activation.

critical_findings.title: Public GitHub repo under a personal handle risks GitHub AUP action and the lookalike-domain generator reads as a phishing/impersonation toolkit to automated scanners
critical_findings.argument: GitHub's Acceptable Use Policies prohibit using the platform to facilitate unsolicited bulk email/spam operations and content whose primary purpose is spamming or violating others' privacy. A public repo that presents as 'a platform to run cold email at scale' plus an AI lookalike-domain generator can trip automated abuse classifiers — the domain generator in particular reads as impersonation/phishing tooling out of context. Publishing it under the personal handle YS-projectcalc ties Yaakov's/EpiphanyMade's reputation to it before entity/ToS clarity and lets history accrue under a personal account. Failure scenario: an abuse scanner flags the repo, GitHub restricts it, and the distribution moat (be THE agent-native repo) is set back or the account is actioned.
critical_findings.fix: Frame the repo as compliance-first B2B email infrastructure with the guardrails (suppression, one-click unsub, per-mailbox caps, AUP) prominent in README/AGENTS.md; scope the lookalike generator explicitly to the sender's OWN brand with a validator that rejects third-party-brand targets; avoid 'spam'/'bulk unsolicited' language; keep all real vendor creds/secrets out of the public repo; and host under an org rather than the personal handle before history accrues.

critical_findings.title: Free no-signup demo / free first-use needs a hard architectural guarantee it can never reach a real adapter, plus abuse rate-limiting even in sandbox
critical_findings.argument: The sandbox-first / dry-run free tier is a genuinely strong spam-abuse mitigation — but two residual holes remain. (1) The abuse-proofing rests on convention (free = sandbox); a single misconfiguration or code path that assigns a real adapter to a free/demo tenant turns the free tier into a spam cannon plus an uncapped vendor-cost incident. (2) An unauthenticated, no-signup demo endpoint that 'runs the full pipeline' is a resource/DoS abuse surface even when sandboxed. Failure scenario: a refactor wires demo jobs through the shared provisioning path, a demo tenant inherits a real Porkbun/Inboxkit adapter, and unauthenticated callers trigger real domain buys / mailbox provisioning at cost.
critical_findings.fix: Enforce at the tenant/provisioning layer that free and demo tenants are STRUCTURALLY incapable of being assigned real adapters — a type-level/config guard, not convention — verified by a test that fails if any real adapter is reachable from a demo context, and ensure demo code paths make no outbound connections to real vendor hosts. Rate-limit and fingerprint the no-signup demo and cap concurrent demo jobs.

missing_items: Per-tenant advertiser physical postal address field + injection into every email footer (CAN-SPAM); EpiphanyMade's own address covers only EpiphanyMade's marketing
Per-tenant verified legal sender identity / entity name (CAN-SPAM sender-identification + customer-is-sender liability posture)

In-body opt-out mechanism — CAN-SPAM requires a conspicuous in-body opt-out, not only the RFC 8058 List-Unsubscribe header; plus honor within 10 business days, opt-out link valid >=30 days, and no sale/transfer of opted-out addresses
Ad-identification of commercial messages where required (CAN-SPAM)
State deceptive-email statute compliance (CA B&P 17529.5 et al.) + subject-line honesty validator to trigger the statutory safe harbor
OFAC / denied-party (sanctions) screening at signup before any real send
Onboarding friction ladder / new-tenant volume ramp before uncapped real sends (business-email verification, Stripe Radar/3DS, list-provenance attestation)
ToS clause: customer designated as the CAN-SPAM 'sender'/advertiser + reps (owns list, valid address, accurate identity, brand rights for lookalikes)
ToS clause: customer indemnification of EpiphanyMade for misuse, recipient claims, and regulatory actions
ToS clause: pass-through of Inboxkit + Google/Microsoft AUP binding the customer as if a direct vendor customer
ToS clause: explicit customer consent to content/metadata monitoring (required to lawfully run complaint-scanning + auto-pause)
ToS clause: no-deliverability/inbox-placement warranty + limitation of liability

AUP prohibited-use list (scraped/purchased lists, third-party impersonation/phishing, unlawful filter-evasion, illegal content, sanctioned parties) WITH a built + tested enforcement/terminate path, not paper-only
Auto-renewal / negative-option disclosure + easy-cancel compliant with state ARLs (e.g., California) + FTC ROSCA (federal Click-to-Cancel rule vacated July 2025; state law + ROSCA still apply)
Privacy policy + processor/DPA addendum defining controller-vs-processor for uploaded lead data (CCPA/CPRA reach even under a US-first plan)
Written vendor confirmation of reseller/multi-tenant permission + exact per-tenant isolation boundary (Phase-A gate, plus one backup vendor)
Marketing disclosures: not-yet-available framing, no deliverability guarantee, honest AI-role claims, and no filter-evasion language
Test/architectural guarantee that free and demo tenants can never be assigned a real vendor adapter
amendments: ROADMAP Phase A: add gate A4 'Vendor reseller-permission + per-tenant isolation-boundary written confirmation (Inboxkit + one backup vendor)' that BLOCKS entry to Phase B — move this off the Phase-0/§16 economics list where it currently sits.

SPEC §0.8 and §7: replace bare 'physical address' with 'per-tenant advertiser physical address injected into every email; EpiphanyMade's address covers only EpiphanyMade's own marketing.' Qualify §7's 'there is no shared reputation pool to protect' as conditional on verified per-tenant org isolation, and add a fallback plan if Inboxkit isolation proves to be only a UI panel.

SPEC §0.8: expand 'US-first' from 'CAN-SPAM' to 'CAN-SPAM + state deceptive-email statutes (CA 17529.5)'; add subject-line-honesty and sender-identity hard validators to the guardrail list, and tighten the §8 lookalike rule into a hard reject of any third-party-brand domain.
SPEC §6: add physical_address + legal_sender_identity params to setup_infrastructure and a compliance_status field to infrastructure_status.

SPEC §11 Phase 0 / ROADMAP B4: expand 'suppression + RFC 8058 one-click unsub' to the FULL CAN-SPAM opt-out flow — in-body opt-out notice, 10-business-day honor window, 30-day link validity, ad-identification, and no sale/transfer of opted-out addresses — and require the sandbox to exercise the real footer/opt-out rendering path so gaps fail a test in test mode.

ROADMAP D3: replace 'ToS, Privacy, AUP' with the explicit clause inventory (customer-is-sender, indemnification, vendor-AUP pass-through, monitoring consent, no-deliverability-warranty, prohibited-use, auto-renewal disclosure, DPA) AND require the AUP enforcement path (auto-pause/terminate) to be built and tested, not merely drafted.
ROADMAP B1/D4: add OFAC/denied-party screening + the new-tenant onboarding friction ladder and volume ramp before uncapped real sends.

SPEC §0.5 / ROADMAP C3: add 'free and demo tenants are structurally incapable of being assigned a real adapter (enforced + covered by a failing-if-violated test)' and rate-limit/fingerprint the no-signup demo.

ROADMAP C2 (marketing site): add explicit not-yet-available + no-deliverability-guarantee disclosure requirements, honest AI-role claims, and a ban on filter-evasion framing of the warmup layer; publish a privacy policy before any waitlist-email collection and make EpiphanyMade's own early-access emails CAN-SPAM-compliant.

ROADMAP C1: frame the public GitHub repo as compliance-first infrastructure (guardrails prominent), scope the lookalike generator to the sender's own brand via validator, and host under an org rather than the YS-projectcalc personal handle before history accrues.
